quotes.verbatim_quote:
For our purposes, the blues will be thought of as a form; it is a twelve measure chord
progression that is based around a I-IV-V7 progression. As you will see, the blues progres-
sion can be very simple or very complex.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines structural foundation of blues form.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Jazz
blues progressions tend to use quite a few substitutions and passing chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core harmonic rule distinguishing jazz blues.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This
lesson begins with a review of the minor pentatonic scale, blues scale and Mixolydian
mode fingerings. They appear here in the key of C, but you should practice transposing
them. Just move to any other root of your choice and work through the same fingerings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes three primary scalar tools for blues soloing with transposition.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
There is a big difference between learning improvisational tools, such as scales, and actu-
ally putting them to use. At first, we tend to organize them into patterns in different areas
of the fingerboard. In actual practice, we usually combine the various tools and utilize the
entire fingerboard. This is because the ideas we hear in our heads don’t always conform to
organized fingering patterns.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Central pedagogical rule: combine scale tools across whole neck.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Study these solos and

memorize the parts that you enjoy so you can use them in your own solos.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practice directive: internalize licks for personal vocabulary.
